DINO share price performance and revenues following the Russian invasion of Ukraine can provide some limited understanding of current market conditions. Shares are likely to spike and plummet alongside crude oil and fuel prices, while revenues will be determined by longer term trends around crack spread.

Most recently, diplomacy between the United States and Iran failed to reach any consensus. Iran continues to restrict traffic in the Strait of Hormuz while the United States plans a naval blockade of Iranian ports. In short, there is no resolution in sight and global energy markets will likely continue to be undersupplied.

Ongoing energy shortages are likely to provide further support for DINO's share price and future earnings. Although I do not expect an orderly or well-behaved market, I recommend those investors with appropriate risk tolerance buy DINO at current market prices.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook: U.S. Blockade On Hormuz Begins - Wall Street Withstands The Pressure
data.description:
By Elior Manier

This weekend marked the start of US-Iran negotiations, which, unsurprisingly, quickly turned sour.

A wave of US and Iranian envoys have come and gone from Islamabad, Pakistan, to maintain the diplomatic attempts – the first round of talks was unfortunately unfruitful.

Numerous disputes have halted discussions, with the US administration eager for an agreement but insisting on demands that the Islamic regime considers too harsh.

WTI crude surged nearly 10% to $104 at the Globex open, sparking pessimism in global stock markets.

The ceasefire is still ongoing, and as long as it continues, there is hope for a more peaceful path ahead.

President Trump recently decided to implement a blockade of blockades in the Strait of Hormuz, with Iran maintaining their leverage on the world's most important 10 km location.

Will this advance a deal? Uncertain, given ongoing threats to end the truce. Prediction markets now estimate the odds of a deal before April 30 at 20%, down from 35% on Friday.

Despite gapping lower at the open, US equities are remaining extremely resilient, with the S&P 500 holding right below unchanged and only the Dow Jones pulling back (~ -0.50%). The moves are still quite timid, definitely not pointing to a return of widespread panic.

While the Middle East situation remains in a status quo, traders are still maintaining optimistic views on the conflict's future. It is wise to keep a close eye on the latest developments to be ready to trade accordingly.

While doubts remain, it is always best to remain ready. Let's look at intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The market picture is quite cloudy, with many of the largest names hanging close to unchanged as participants are still uncertain on what to do next.

Producer manufacturing is a laggard, while tech (particularly electronics) is attempting a rebound.

Once again, sectorial trading seems quite compromised amid recent confusion; hence, it could be more strategic to either focus on individual stocks or index trading to avoid getting caught in crosswinds.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow Jones Index gapped lower at the open, struggling the most out of the three main US benchmarks. However, sellers could not continue their short-lived dominance, and a rebound attempt is already on the way.

For the short term, look at 48,000 for immediate bull/bear guidance, as this will be a target for the ongoing rebound.

  • Rejecting it points to more downside ahead, pointing towards 47,000
  • Breaking back above 48,000 would look to retest the past week's highs (48,300) – Above this, the action turns long-term bullish.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Momentum Pivot at 48,000 (short-term bearish below)
  • March 4 resistance 48,250 - 48,300 (bull above)
  • Mini-resistance 48,700
  • Major Resistance – 49,000 to 49,200

Support Levels

  • Major Pivotal Support 47,400 to 47,600 (Session lows)
  • War Resistance now Key Support 47,000 +/- 100 Points (Bearish below)
  • March 8 War lows Resistance now Support 46,300
  • 45,700 to 45,900 August Support
  • January 2025 Highs 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq remains persistent within its 25,000 to 25,250 area, forming a short-term consolidation range.

The longer the action stays in that zone, the higher the chances of an upside breakout.

Nonetheless, this zone is a longer-run resistance; hence, any bearish outflow could see heavy momentum – keep track of sentiment for more precise execution.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Key Resistance 25,000 to 25,250 (top at 25,250!)
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb Range resistance
  • Major resistance 25,700 to 25,850

Support Levels

  • 24,750 to 24,900 Momentum Pivot
  • 24,450 to 24,550 Pivotal Support
  • Feb Range Support 24,150 to 24,200
  • Major 2026 Pivotal Support 23,800 to 24,000
  • August 2025 Support 23,500 to 23,650
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is already running back towards its Friday highs as traders maintain further hopes for continued talks.

Breaking 6,840 puts the index on a bullish short-term outlook. Failing to breach this level maintains a more range-bound outlook ahead.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Early March Resistance 6,820 to 6,840 (testing)
  • Key Resistance Zone 6,880 to 6,900
  • Previous ATH Resistance 6,945 to 6,975

Support Levels

  • Major Momentum Pivot 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,680 to 6,700 Pivotal Support (4H 200-period MA)
  • 6,580 to 6,610 Support
  • 4H 50-period MA 6,550
  • 6,490 to 6,520 October lows
  • 6,300 psychological level (War lows)

The narrative is still confusing, so keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the week to stay ahead of the game.

data.title: EIM: Discount Widens Back To A More Attractive Level
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Eaton Vance Municipal Bond Fund (EIM) has been under recent pressure once again as long-term rates start to rise and short-term rates are expected to remain elevated. As a closed-end fund that invests in municipal bonds, it is highly sensitive to interest rates, and that is compounded by its leverage. Given the outlook was the expectation for further rate cuts from the Fed, with those on hold due to rising oil prices likely to keep inflation sticky even longer, that can continue to keep its borrowing costs elevated.

However, some positive news is that the fund has also seen its discount widen since our last update. Given the added pressures on the fund, combined with that, it can make it a more interesting time to consider adding to this position than it was previously.

EIM Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.36
  • Discount/Premium: -6.70%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.16%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.17%
  • Leverage: 30.66%
  • Managed Assets: $787.28 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

EIM's investment objective is to “provide current income exempt from federal income tax.”

They intend to achieve this through:

... at least 80% of the Fund’s net assets will be invested in municipal obligations, the interest on which is exempt from federal income tax, including the alternative minimum tax (“AMT”), and that are rated A or better by Moody’s Investors Service, Inc. (“Moody’s”), S&P Global Ratings (“S&P”) or Fitch Ratings (“Fitch”). The foregoing 80% policy may not be changed without shareholder approval. Under normal market conditions, the Fund expects to be fully invested (at least 95% of its net assets) in accordance with its investment objective. The Fund may invest up to 20% of its net assets in municipal obligations rated BBB/Baa or below (or unrated obligations deemed by the Fund’s adviser, Eaton Vance Management (“Eaton Vance”), to be of equivalent quality), provided that not more than 15% of its net assets may be invested in municipal obligations rated below B (or unrated obligations deemed by Eaton Vance to be of equivalent quality) and may invest up to 20% of its net assets in bonds on which the interest is subject to the AMT. When a municipal obligation is split rated (meaning rated in different categories by Moody’s, S&P, or Fitch) the Fund will deem the higher rating to apply.

When including the fund's interest expenses, the total expense ratio comes up to 2.88%. Since the last annual report, financing costs would have come down even further, but we'll be discussing that further below. While leverage has the positive impact of potentially higher returns moving forward, it also comes with greater overall risk to the downside and increased volatility. That can lead to what would otherwise be a fairly conservative municipal bond portfolio, not being appropriate for every investor.

Discount Widens Driving Flat Results

The fund has delivered about flat total returns since our last update, but it actually would have been better in terms of its total NAV return results. The total NAV return results being that of its actual underlying portfolio.

This is because the fund was sporting around a 2% discount, a historically narrow level, before it has now widened back out. In our last update, we were also discussing the tender offer, which is likely the key catalyst keeping the discount at such a narrow level. In fact, the day we ended up covering the name, it was surging over 4%.

Even further, over the longer term, the fund's discount is incredibly narrow compared to its average level. That has made it relatively less appealing as a choice for putting capital to work today.

If I were to have to guess, I suspect we might see the price fall back down after Friday's unusual surge. Trading volume was a bit higher than average, but it wasn't anything too dramatic at 1.32x the average (~195k compared to ~148k daily).

Well, my guess proved to be right as the share price did fall quite dramatically shortly after. It was then that the latest pressures on this muni-focused CEF put added downward force on the fund's discount. Those pressures are coming from a rising 10 Year U.S. Treasury Rate on the back of the Iran war and rising oil prices. This has been enough to push it back toward its historical discount level—but not exactly over the level yet. Though more recently it briefly did breach the 9% level, thanks to the more significant volatility we've seen lately.

Given the share price pressures from both higher interest rates and discount widening, it makes it a more interesting choice for investors to consider adding at this time.

High Distribution From Munis, But Watching Coverage

The fund's current distribution rate works out to 6.16%, and a NAV rate of 5.75%. That's quite high for municipal bonds, and that can be attributed a bit to the leverage, as investors expect a higher rate when investing in a leveraged fund.

However, when looking at the fund's leverage, it is sensitive to short-term rates as its borrowing costs are through floating-rate notes. In our prior update, we were discussing the positive outlook on this front from further rate cuts. Since that write-up, we did get another 75 basis points of cuts, which should have helped to alleviate the borrowing costs further. However, the outlook for further rate cuts heading into 2026 has now been put on hold.

We noted previously that the fund was not covering its payout entirely from net investment income either. Meaning that they were choosing to pay an elevated rate, likely to keep interest in the fund. This isn't anything new to municipal bond CEFs, which most regularly overpay what they actually earn.

Diving into the latest UNII report from the Eaton Vance fixed-income CEFs, we can see that EIM's latest quarterly coverage sits at around 79%.

That's actually quite the improvement from the 70% we noted from the fund's semi-annual report, for the period ending March 31, 2025.

For the full fiscal year period ending September 30, 2025, we also saw distribution coverage sit similarly at around 70.5%. Therefore, we are seeing those next 75 basis points of cuts in late 2025 starting to kick in during the latest rolling three-month UNII report.

That's the positive, but we are now at a point where this will most likely not see any further added boosts higher. At least, not material boosts unless they start to turn over their portfolio to higher-yielding holdings. As they focus primarily on higher-quality municipal bonds, I wouldn't anticipate seeing any sort of dramatic shift unless they change the investment policy of the fund.

EIM's Portfolio

The fund's average effective duration comes in at 6.68 years, and its leverage-adjusted duration comes in at 9.65 years. Thus, highlighting just how interest rate sensitive the fund's underlying portfolio is. This is primarily driven by the high-quality exposure of muni funds and being able to get away with extended maturities. In this case, the average maturity comes in at 20.34 years, across its 175 portfolio holdings.

For the investors who have to worry about the alternative minimum tax, the fund lists that it has 9.63% that is exposed to AMT.

In looking at the top holdings, it isn't too heavily concentrated in any specific holding. The two largest holdings could be considered somewhat relatively overweight relative to the others that follow, but we quickly drop to around the 1% allocation range by the end of the top ten.

This is relatively more concentrated compared to its high-yield CEF peers out there, but given the higher quality and the significantly reduced expectations for defaults, this would appear fine. One of the reasons why high-yield funds hold hundreds and hundreds, or even into the thousands, of different holdings is precisely because of the higher risk for default. If they spread their portfolio more diversely, that limits the downside risks of only a handful of positions causing too much damage.

Conclusion

Thanks to the increased volatility lately, we've seen EIM's discount widen back out. There is a more pessimistic outlook with higher rates, and that comes in the form of higher short-term rates as well. Higher short-term rates due to the Fed likely holding rates steady in 2026 mean borrowing costs on the leverage will stay elevated. This was after the outlook was initially for continued cuts, which had seen NII coverage increase quite meaningfully over the last couple of years. That said, when the fund, or any investment, is under pressure, that is often the time to consider adding for long-term investors.
